topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My hands, already trembling, fumbled with the mouse, sending the screen blurring. The headline, the byline—it was all wrong. My meticulously crafted piece on urban gardening, my voice, my analysis, had been plastered under the name of some stranger. A strangled sound escaped my throat, a pathetic little squeak that died in the quiet office. I stood up so fast my chair scraped violently across the hardwood floor. My breath hitched, shallow and ragged, as I reread the attribution. It was impossible. A complete fabrication. My mind spun, a frantic, dizzying carousel of disbelief and sheer, unadulterated shock. I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to physically squeeze the impossible reality out of my vision.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen until the pixels swam into meaningless streaks of light. The air in the room felt thick, suffocating. My chest tightened, a hard band cinching my ribs, making every inhale a conscious, painful effort. When I finally managed to focus, the words swam before my eyes—*By: Eleanor Vance*. Eleanor Vance. Not me. The blood seemed to drain from my face, leaving a cold, clammy sheen. I began pacing the small space between my desk and the window, my steps quick and uneven, a caged animal desperate for an exit. My voice, when I finally spoke, was a reedy whisper, thin and brittle. "No. No, this can't be happening. This is a mistake. A cruel, elaborate mistake." I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles were white, fighting the urge to throw the entire laptop across the room.

[story 3]

It started as a faint prickle on the back of my neck, a sense of profound wrongness, but by the time I clicked on the final draft, the sensation had escalated into a full-body tremor. My vision tunneled; the room seemed to tilt on its axis. The words confirmed the nightmare: my unique perspective, my research, someone else’s property. I dropped the keyboard with a clatter that sounded deafening in the silence. I sank into the nearest chair, pulling my knees up to my chest, wrapping my arms tightly around myself as if to contain the sudden, overwhelming surge of panic. I started rocking, a small, jerky motion, my jaw working silently as I tried desperately to reconcile the reality of the published article with the undeniable truth screaming in my mind. Every nerve ending felt exposed, vibrating with a high-pitched tension that made my skin crawl.
